caseStudies.title: Wong's Bakery — April 20X5
caseStudies.text:
In April 20X5, Wong's Bakery was opened with the following transactions:

a. On April 1, Miss Wong, the owner, invested $100,000 in cash and a convection oven at a cost of $24,000. The oven is expected to have a useful life of 10 years with zero disposal value. The straight-line method is used to calculate the oven's depreciation expense.

b. On April 1, the bakery paid $15,000 for the shop furniture. The furniture is expected to be used for 5 years with zero disposal value. The straight-line method is used to calculate the furniture's depreciation expense.

c. On April 1, the bakery paid $2,000 for business licences and permits. These are necessary costs for running a bakery and are one-off expenses.

d. On April 2, the bakery paid $18,000 for six-month rent, starting from 1 April.

e. On April 2, the company purchased $2,000 baking materials on credit. At the end of April, a physical count showed that $300 of baking materials were left.

f. On April 15, the company paid $12,000 for insurance premiums covering 12 months, effective April 15.

g. On April 20, the company settled the payment for baking materials purchased on April 2.

h. In April, the company received $12,000 cash for bread and cakes sold during the month. Meanwhile, it collected $1,000 in advance to deliver a custom birthday cake in May.

i. On April 30, the company received April's utility bill of $1,500 and planned to settle it in May.

j. On April 30, a payment of $3,500 was made for salaries in April.

caseStudies.title: Green Foods Ltd — Income Statements 20X3 & 20X4
caseStudies.text:
Green Foods Ltd is a mid-sized company specialising in the processing and packaging of organic vegetables. The company sources vegetables from local farmers and then processes them into canned products. Market supply and climate conditions significantly affect the purchasing cost of vegetables.

INCOME STATEMENT — For the Financial Year Ended 31 December

                              20X4           20X3
Sales                     $2,000,000     $1,800,000
Cost of sales            ($1,100,000)     ($900,000)
Gross profit                $900,000       $900,000
Selling & distribution    ($420,000)     ($400,000)
Research & development     ($10,000)      ($80,000)
Administrative expenses    ($30,000)      ($30,000)
Finance costs              ($10,000)       ($4,000)
Others                      ($2,000)       ($1,500)
Profit before income tax    $428,000       $384,500
Income tax                  ($72,760)      ($65,365)
Net profit after tax        $355,240       $319,135

Liquidity ratios (given):

| Ratio          | 20X4 | 20X3 | 20X2 | 20X1 | 20X0 | Industry 20X4 | Industry 5-yr avg |
|----------------|------|------|------|------|------|---------------|-------------------|
| Current ratio  |  2.1 |  2.2 |  2.4 |  2.5 |  2.6 |           1.9 |               2.0 |
| Acid-test ratio|  0.8 |  0.9 |  0.8 |  0.7 |  0.8 |           0.9 |               1.0 |

caseStudies.title: Convenience Ltd — Vending Machine Expansion
caseStudies.text:
Convenience Ltd operates and services snack vending machines in restaurants, metro stations, canteens, etc. The machines are leased from the manufacturer, and the company must also rent the space to locate the machines. The following details outline the predicted monthly expenses and data for a proposed expansion involving 20 machines.

Fixed costs:
  Machine rent (20 machines × $3,000)    $60,000
  Space rent (20 machines × $2,000)      $40,000
  Wages to service the 20 machines        $5,000
  Other fixed costs                       $15,000
  Total fixed costs                      $120,000

Other data:
  Budgeted monthly sales of snacks:  50,000 units
  Selling price:                     $5 per unit
  Cost of snack:                     20% of selling price

Option 1 (current): Fixed machine rent of $60,000 per month.
Option 2 (proposed): Variable machine rent at 25% of sales; no fixed machine rent.

caseStudies.title: XYZ Company — Cash Budget April–June 20X5
caseStudies.text:
XYZ Company's operations began in March 20X5. Its sales in March were $300,000. The ending cash balance for March is $10,000. During March, the company approached the bank for a $200,000 short-term loan to be made on April 1 and repaid on June 30. The bank requires monthly cash budgets from April to June.

Budgeted sales:
  April   $320,000
  May     $350,000
  June    $360,000
  July    $340,000

Additional information:
• 20% of monthly sales are cash sales; 80% are credit sales.
• 60% of credit sales are collected in the first month following the sale; the remaining 40% are collected in the second month.
• Purchases each month equal the cost of goods sold of the following month. COGS is 50% of sales. All purchases are on credit, settled in the first month following purchase.
• Operating expenses are 30% of sales, including a fixed monthly depreciation of $25,000. All other operating expenses are paid when incurred.
• The annual interest rate on the bank loan is 6%. Both principal and interest are paid on June 30 (maturity date).

questions.label: Question 1a
questions.text:
For each transaction (a) to (j), explain its impact on the accounts in the expanded accounting equation (Assets, Liabilities, Equity, Revenues, Expenses) for Wong's Bakery in April 20X5.

List each account affected separately. Use this format:
  Item | Account Type | Amount ($) | Increase or Decrease

Note: For items with useful lives (a, b), include the April depreciation adjustment. For items involving prepayments (d, f) and inventory (e), show the net April impact after period-end adjustments.
questions.modelAnswer:
a. Owner's investment — Cash + Oven:
   Assets (Cash) | $100,000 | Increase
   Assets (Convection Oven) | $24,000 | Increase
   Equity (Owner's Capital) | $124,000 | Increase
   Depreciation adjustment (oven, 1 month): $24,000 ÷ 10 ÷ 12 = $200
   Assets (Accumulated Depreciation – Oven) | $200 | Increase
   Expenses (Depreciation Expense) | $200 | Increase

b. Shop furniture:
   Assets (Shop Furniture) | $15,000 | Increase
   Assets (Cash) | $15,000 | Decrease
   Depreciation adjustment (furniture, 1 month): $15,000 ÷ 5 ÷ 12 = $250
   Assets (Accumulated Depreciation – Furniture) | $250 | Increase
   Expenses (Depreciation Expense) | $250 | Increase

c. Business licences (one-off expense):
   Expenses (Licence Expense) | $2,000 | Increase
   Assets (Cash) | $2,000 | Decrease

d. Six-month rent from April 1 ($18,000 ÷ 6 = $3,000/month):
   Assets (Cash) | $18,000 | Decrease
   Assets (Prepaid Rent) | $15,000 | Increase  [net: $18,000 paid – $3,000 used]
   Expenses (Rent Expense) | $3,000 | Increase

e. Baking materials on credit ($2,000); $300 unused at month-end; $1,700 used:
   Assets (Baking Materials/Inventory) | $300 | Increase  [net remaining]
   Liabilities (Accounts Payable) | $2,000 | Increase
   Expenses (Baking Materials Expense) | $1,700 | Increase

f. Insurance 12 months from April 15 ($12,000 ÷ 12 × 0.5 = $500 for half of April):
   Assets (Cash) | $12,000 | Decrease
   Assets (Prepaid Insurance) | $11,500 | Increase  [net: $12,000 – $500]
   Expenses (Insurance Expense) | $500 | Increase

g. Settled accounts payable for baking materials:
   Liabilities (Accounts Payable) | $2,000 | Decrease
   Assets (Cash) | $2,000 | Decrease

h. Cash sales $12,000 + $1,000 advance for May birthday cake:
   Assets (Cash) | $13,000 | Increase
   Revenue (Sales Revenue) | $12,000 | Increase
   Liabilities (Unearned Revenue) | $1,000 | Increase

i. Utility bill received, to be paid in May:
   Expenses (Utility Expense) | $1,500 | Increase
   Liabilities (Utilities Payable) | $1,500 | Increase

j. Salaries paid:
   Expenses (Salary Expense) | $3,500 | Increase
   Assets (Cash) | $3,500 | Decrease
keyPoints: Item (a): three accounts — Cash increase, Oven increase, Equity increase; plus depreciation $200
Item (b): Furniture increase, Cash decrease; plus depreciation $250
Item (c): Expense increase $2,000, Cash decrease — licences are expensed immediately (one-off)
Item (d): Cash decrease $18,000, Prepaid Rent increase $15,000, Rent Expense increase $3,000 (1/6 of $18,000)
Item (e): Baking Materials $300, AP $2,000, Expense $1,700 — must split purchased vs used
Item (f): Cash decrease $12,000, Prepaid Insurance $11,500, Insurance Expense $500 (0.5/12 of $12,000)
Item (g): AP decrease $2,000, Cash decrease $2,000 — settling the liability
Item (h): Cash increase $13,000, Revenue $12,000, Unearned Revenue $1,000 — advance is a liability not revenue
Item (i): Expense $1,500, Utilities Payable $1,500 — accrued, not paid
Item (j): Salary Expense $3,500, Cash decrease $3,500

questions.label: Question 1b
questions.text:
Calculate:
(i) The profit or loss of Wong's Bakery for April 20X5.
(ii) The balance of equity as of April 30, 20X5.
questions.modelAnswer:
(i) Profit/(Loss) for April 20X5:

Revenue:
  Sales Revenue                          $12,000

Expenses:
  Licence Expense                         $2,000
  Rent Expense (1/6 × $18,000)            $3,000
  Baking Materials Expense                $1,700
  Insurance Expense (0.5/12 × $12,000)     $500
  Utility Expense                         $1,500
  Salary Expense                          $3,500
  Depreciation – Oven ($24,000÷10÷12)      $200
  Depreciation – Furniture ($15,000÷5÷12)  $250
  Total Expenses                         $12,650

Net Loss = $12,000 – $12,650 = $(650)

(ii) Equity at April 30, 20X5:

  Opening equity (new business)              $0
  Add: Owner's investment (cash + oven)  $124,000
  Add/(Less): Net loss                     ($650)
  Equity at April 30                    $123,350
keyPoints: Revenue is only $12,000 (cash sales) — the $1,000 advance is Unearned Revenue, not income
8 expense items must be included: licences, rent ($3,000 not $18,000), materials used ($1,700), insurance ($500), utilities, salaries, and TWO depreciation charges
Oven depreciation: $24,000 ÷ 10 years ÷ 12 months = $200
Furniture depreciation: $15,000 ÷ 5 years ÷ 12 months = $250
Net result is a loss of $650
Equity = $124,000 investment – $650 net loss = $123,350

questions.label: Question 1c
questions.text: Calculate the asset turnover ratio for Wong's Bakery for April 20X5, and comment on the ratio. Assume the benchmark asset turnover ratio for this industry is 1.0. Round your answer to two decimal places.
questions.modelAnswer:
Total assets at April 30, 20X5:
  Cash: $100,000 – $15,000 – $2,000 – $18,000 – $12,000 – $2,000 + $13,000 – $3,500 = $60,500
  Convection Oven (net of depreciation): $24,000 – $200 = $23,800
  Shop Furniture (net): $15,000 – $250 = $14,750
  Baking Materials: $300
  Prepaid Rent: $15,000
  Prepaid Insurance: $11,500
  Total Assets = $125,850

Asset Turnover = Sales ÷ Total Assets
             = $12,000 ÷ $125,850
             = 0.10 (rounded to 2 decimal places)

Comment: The asset turnover ratio of 0.10 is significantly below the industry benchmark of 1.0, indicating that the bakery is generating only $0.10 of revenue for every $1.00 of assets. However, this is expected for a business in its first month of operation — the bakery has made substantial upfront investments in equipment, prepayments, and capital, but has had very limited time to generate sales. The ratio should improve considerably as the business matures and monthly revenue grows relative to its asset base.
keyPoints: Formula: Asset Turnover = Sales ÷ Total Assets
Sales = $12,000 (April only)
Total assets correctly calculated at ~$125,850
Ratio = 0.10 (accept 0.09–0.10 range due to rounding)
Comment must note: below benchmark 1.0; reason is new business with high assets but low revenue; expected to improve

questions.label: Question 2a
questions.text:
Using the ACC202 formula sheet, calculate the following profitability ratios for Green Foods Ltd. Round all answers to two decimal places.

(i) Gross margin for 20X4 and 20X3.
(ii) Profit margin for 20X4 and 20X3.
(iii) Sales growth in 20X4.
(iv) Profit growth in 20X4.
questions.modelAnswer:
(i) Gross Margin = Gross Profit ÷ Sales
  20X4: $900,000 ÷ $2,000,000 = 45.00%
  20X3: $900,000 ÷ $1,800,000 = 50.00%

(ii) Profit Margin = Net Profit After Tax ÷ Sales
  20X4: $355,240 ÷ $2,000,000 = 17.76%
  20X3: $319,135 ÷ $1,800,000 = 17.73%

(iii) Sales Growth = (This Year Sales – Last Year Sales) ÷ Last Year Sales
  = ($2,000,000 – $1,800,000) ÷ $1,800,000
  = $200,000 ÷ $1,800,000
  = 11.11%

(iv) Profit Growth = (This Year Profit – Last Year Profit) ÷ Last Year Profit
  = ($355,240 – $319,135) ÷ $319,135
  = $36,105 ÷ $319,135
  = 11.31%
keyPoints: Profit margin 20X4 = 17.76%; 20X3 = 17.73%
Sales growth = 11.11%
Profit growth = 11.31%
Must use Net Profit After Tax for profit margin (not profit before tax)

questions.label: Question 2b
questions.text: Analyse the company's profitability based on the ratios calculated in Question 2a and the income statement provided. Your answer should cover trends, causes, and implications.
questions.modelAnswer:
Green Foods Ltd shows mixed profitability signals in 20X4.

Gross margin declined significantly from 50.00% in 20X3 to 45.00% in 20X4. Cost of sales rose by $200,000 (from $900k to $1,100k) while sales grew by only $200,000. This reflects the case description — market supply and climate conditions pushed up vegetable purchasing costs, squeezing the gross margin despite strong top-line growth. This is a structural risk: input costs are largely outside management's control.

Despite the gross margin decline, profit margin remained virtually flat at 17.73% (20X3) vs 17.76% (20X4). This is because research and development expense fell sharply from $80,000 to $10,000, which largely offset the increase in cost of sales. While this stabilised the profit margin in the short term, the dramatic reduction in R&D is a concern — it may indicate underinvestment in product development and future competitiveness.

Sales growth of 11.11% is positive, reflecting the company's ability to expand revenue. Profit growth of 11.31% marginally outpaced sales growth, which appears healthy on the surface but is largely attributable to the R&D cut rather than operational improvements.

Finance costs doubled from $4,000 to $10,000, suggesting increased borrowing. If sales growth is being debt-funded, this adds financial risk. Overall, profitability appears stable but fragile — dependent on continued cost-cutting and vulnerable to further input cost increases.
keyPoints: Gross margin fell from 50% to 45% — link to rising COGS/vegetable input costs
Profit margin essentially flat — explain why (R&D expense dropped $80k to $10k)
Must note that R&D reduction is a concern for long-term sustainability
Sales growth 11.11% is positive
Finance costs doubled — signal of increased borrowing/financial risk
Answer should cover: trend, cause, implication (not just state the numbers)

questions.label: Question 2c
questions.text:
The following liquidity ratios are provided for Green Foods Ltd over the last five years, along with industry benchmarks.

| Ratio           | 20X4 | 20X3 | 20X2 | 20X1 | 20X0 | Industry 20X4 | Industry 5-yr avg |
|-----------------|------|------|------|------|------|---------------|-------------------|
| Current ratio   |  2.1 |  2.2 |  2.4 |  2.5 |  2.6 |           1.9 |               2.0 |
| Acid-test ratio |  0.8 |  0.9 |  0.8 |  0.7 |  0.8 |           0.9 |               1.0 |

Analyse the company's liquidity based on the ratios given.
questions.modelAnswer:
Green Foods Ltd's liquidity presents a dual picture: adequate on current ratio metrics but concerning on the quick ratio.

The current ratio has been declining steadily from 2.6 in 20X0 to 2.1 in 20X4. Despite this five-year downward trend, the current ratio still exceeds the industry average (1.9) and the 5-year industry average (2.0), meaning the company retains more short-term assets than peers relative to current liabilities. Short-term liquidity is not immediately at risk.

However, the acid-test (quick) ratio tells a more concerning story. At 0.8 in 20X4, it is below the industry average (0.9) and significantly below the industry 5-year average (1.0). This indicates the company cannot fully cover current liabilities with its most liquid assets (cash, receivables) — it relies heavily on inventory to maintain its current ratio. As a food processing company, inventory may not be quickly convertible to cash.

The large gap between the current ratio (2.1) and acid-test ratio (0.8) confirms substantial inventory holdings. If inventory becomes obsolete or difficult to sell (e.g., seasonal spoilage of organic vegetables), liquidity could deteriorate rapidly.

Overall: while the company is not in immediate liquidity distress, the declining trend and below-industry quick ratio warrant close monitoring. Management should focus on reducing inventory levels and improving receivables collection to strengthen liquid asset coverage.
keyPoints: Current ratio declining trend (2.6 to 2.1) but still above industry 1.9 — adequate
Acid-test ratio 0.8 below industry 0.9 and 5yr avg 1.0 — concerning
Large gap between current (2.1) and acid-test (0.8) = significant inventory dependence
Link to business context: organic food company, inventory risk (spoilage, seasonal)
Overall assessment: not in crisis but declining trend and inventory reliance need monitoring

questions.label: Question 3a
questions.text:
Apply cost-volume-profit analysis to calculate:
(i) Contribution margin per unit of snack.
(ii) Contribution margin ratio.
(iii) Break-even point in units.
(iv) Break-even point in dollars.
(v) Margin of safety in percentage at budgeted sales of 50,000 units.
questions.modelAnswer:
Given: Selling price = $5/unit; Variable cost = 20% × $5 = $1/unit; Total fixed costs = $120,000; Budgeted sales = 50,000 units

(i) Contribution Margin per unit = Selling Price – Variable Cost
   = $5 – $1 = $4 per unit

(ii) CM Ratio = CM per unit ÷ Selling Price
   = $4 ÷ $5 = 0.80 = 80%

(iii) Break-even point (units) = Fixed Costs ÷ CM per unit
   = $120,000 ÷ $4 = 30,000 units

(iv) Break-even point ($) = Fixed Costs ÷ CM Ratio
   = $120,000 ÷ 0.80 = $150,000

(v) Margin of Safety % = (Budgeted Sales – BEP Units) ÷ Budgeted Sales × 100
   = (50,000 – 30,000) ÷ 50,000 × 100
   = 20,000 ÷ 50,000 × 100 = 40%
keyPoints: Variable cost = 20% × $5 = $1/unit (not $5)
CM per unit = $4
CM ratio = 80%
BEP units = 30,000
BEP dollars = $150,000

questions.label: Question 3b
questions.text: Calculate the degree of operating leverage (DOL) at the budgeted sales level of 50,000 units. Explain what the degree of operating leverage means for Convenience Ltd.
questions.modelAnswer:
At 50,000 units:
  Revenue = 50,000 × $5 = $250,000
  Variable costs = 50,000 × $1 = $50,000
  Contribution Margin = $200,000
  Fixed costs = $120,000
  Operating Income = $200,000 – $120,000 = $80,000

DOL = Contribution Margin ÷ Operating Income
    = $200,000 ÷ $80,000
    = 2.5

Meaning: A degree of operating leverage of 2.5 means that for every 1% change in sales, operating income will change by 2.5% (in the same direction). For example, a 10% increase in sales would increase operating income by 25%; equally, a 10% decrease in sales would reduce operating income by 25%.

Convenience Ltd has high fixed costs ($120,000 out of $250,000 revenue = 48% of sales), which creates significant operating leverage. This amplifies both upside and downside — the company benefits greatly from sales growth but is vulnerable to sales declines.
keyPoints: DOL = CM ÷ Operating Income = $200,000 ÷ $80,000 = 2.5
Explanation must include: 1% change in sales → 2.5% change in operating income
Must explain the direction (same direction as sales change)
Should link to high fixed costs creating leverage effect
Must mention risk: amplifies both upside (sales increase) and downside (sales decline)

questions.label: Question 3c
questions.text:
The machine manufacturer offers Option 2: a variable machine rent at 25% of sales, with no fixed machine rent (currently $60,000 fixed under Option 1).

(i) Calculate the sales level (in units) at which the company is indifferent between Option 1 and Option 2.
(ii) Use relevant costs to decide whether the company should accept Option 2 at its budgeted sales of 50,000 units.
questions.modelAnswer:
(i) Indifference Point:

Under Option 1:
  Total Fixed Costs = $120,000 (includes $60,000 machine rent)
  Variable cost per unit = $1 (snack cost only)
  Operating Income = 4Q – 120,000  [CM = $4]

Under Option 2:
  Fixed machine rent removed: Fixed Costs = $120,000 – $60,000 = $60,000
  Variable machine rent = 25% × $5 = $1.25 per unit
  New VC per unit = $1.00 + $1.25 = $2.25
  New CM per unit = $5.00 – $2.25 = $2.75
  Operating Income = 2.75Q – 60,000

At indifference point, OI Option 1 = OI Option 2:
  4Q – 120,000 = 2.75Q – 60,000
  1.25Q = 60,000
  Q = 48,000 units

(ii) Decision at 50,000 units (above indifference point of 48,000):

Option 1 OI = (50,000 × $4) – $120,000 = $200,000 – $120,000 = $80,000
Option 2 OI = (50,000 × $2.75) – $60,000 = $137,500 – $60,000 = $77,500

Option 1 generates $2,500 more operating income at budgeted sales.

Decision: Reject Option 2. At the budgeted sales level of 50,000 units (which exceeds the indifference point of 48,000 units), Option 1 (fixed rent) is more profitable. The company should retain the current fixed machine rent arrangement.
keyPoints: Indifference equation: 4Q – 120,000 = 2.75Q – 60,000
Indifference point = 48,000 units
At 50,000 units: Option 1 OI $80,000 > Option 2 OI $77,500
Decision: Reject Option 2 (keep fixed rent) because 50,000 > 48,000 units
Must show working for both OI calculations

questions.label: Question 4
questions.text:
Present cash budgets for XYZ Company for April, May and June 20X5, assuming the bank loan of $200,000 is approved on April 1.

Your budget should clearly show:
- Cash receipts (cash sales + credit collections)
- Cash disbursements (purchase payments + cash operating expenses)
- Financing (loan received, principal repayment, interest)
- Opening and closing cash balances for each month
questions.modelAnswer:
STEP 1 — Sales breakdown:
  March: $300,000 | April: $320,000 | May: $350,000 | June: $360,000 | July: $340,000
  Cash sales (20%): Mar $60k | Apr $64k | May $70k | Jun $72k
  Credit sales (80%): Mar $240k | Apr $256k | May $280k | Jun $288k

STEP 2 — Credit collections:
  60% collected month after sale; 40% two months after:
  April collections from March credit: 60% × $240k = $144,000
  May collections:
    From April credit (60%): 60% × $256k = $153,600
    From March credit (40%): 40% × $240k = $96,000
  June collections:
    From May credit (60%): 60% × $280k = $168,000
    From April credit (40%): 40% × $256k = $102,400

STEP 3 — Purchases (= COGS of next month = 50% × next month sales); paid month after:
  March purchases (for April COGS): 50% × $320k = $160,000 → paid April
  April purchases (for May COGS): 50% × $350k = $175,000 → paid May
  May purchases (for June COGS): 50% × $360k = $180,000 → paid June

STEP 4 — Cash operating expenses (30% of sales – $25,000 depreciation):
  April: 30% × $320k – $25k = $96k – $25k = $71,000
  May:   30% × $350k – $25k = $105k – $25k = $80,000
  June:  30% × $360k – $25k = $108k – $25k = $83,000

STEP 5 — Interest: $200,000 × 6% × (3/12) = $3,000 (paid June 30)

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CASH BUDGET — XYZ Company, April to June 20X5
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
                              April       May        June
Opening Cash Balance        $10,000   $187,000   $251,600

ADD: Cash Receipts
  Cash sales (20% of sales)  $64,000    $70,000    $72,000
  Credit collected (60% prior)$144,000  $153,600   $168,000
  Credit collected (40% prior)      –    $96,000   $102,400
  Bank loan received         $200,000         –          –
Total Cash Available        $418,000   $506,600   $594,000

LESS: Cash Disbursements
  Purchases paid             $160,000   $175,000   $180,000
  Cash operating expenses     $71,000    $80,000    $83,000
Total Disbursements         $231,000   $255,000   $263,000

Excess / (Shortage)         $187,000   $251,600   $331,000

FINANCING
  Loan repayment (principal)        –          –  ($200,000)
  Interest paid                     –          –    ($3,000)

Closing Cash Balance        $187,000   $251,600   $128,000
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Key notes:
• Depreciation ($25,000/month) is EXCLUDED — non-cash item
• No March credit collection in April (company started in March, only 1 month of credit sales)
• Interest = $200,000 × 6% × 3/12 = $3,000 paid at June 30 maturity
keyPoints: Cash sales: 20% of each month's sales
April credit collections: only March credit (60%) — no 2-month-prior credit as company started in March
May: April credit (60% × $256k = $153,600) + March credit (40% × $240k = $96,000)
Purchases = 50% of NEXT month's sales, paid the following month
March purchases ($160k) paid in April; April purchases ($175k) in May; May purchases ($180k) in June
Cash opex = 30% of sales MINUS $25,000 depreciation each month
Bank loan $200k received April 1; repaid June 30 with $3,000 interest
Closing balances: April $187,000 | May $251,600 | June $128,000
